title: Warhammer 40,000 edition timeline
description: Major game releases of Warhammer 40,000 from the first miniature range (Rogue Trader, 1987) to the current edition. Built as the spine of the Codex Armorum historical record. Each edition record is the parent milestone; per-model releases in model_catalogue_manual.json hang off these via release_year and the era ranges below.
notes: Day-precise launch dates are given where Games Workshop announced them. Earlier editions use month or year precision only, matching what is reliably documented. Fields marked projected are not confirmed by Games Workshop.

editions.name: Rogue Trader
editions.launch_box_note: No starter set. Released as a single hardcover rulebook. Reprint still sold at Warhammer World.
editions.rules_summary: Foundational ruleset. A narrative, gamesmaster-led hybrid of skirmish wargame and roleplaying game with very detailed per-model profiles and open-ended wargear. Closer to a large skirmish than the platoon-scale battle game the line later became.
key_changes: Deeply detailed wargear and character profiles
iconic_models: Early Citadel metal Space Marine, Ork and Imperial Guard range
editions.narrative_hook: Establishes the 41st Millennium and the grimdark setting.
editions.lineage_note: Setting and mechanics trace partly to Laserburn (Tabletop Games, 1980, by Bryan Ansell).

editions.name: 2nd Edition
editions.launch_box_note: First time a single boxed set let a new player buy one product and play out of the box. RRP about 34.99 GBP / 59.99 USD.
editions.rules_summary: Structured boxed game built on character-driven play, wargear and strategy cards, a card-based psychic phase and overwatch. Supported by the Dark Millennium expansion.
editions.narrative_hook: Colourful, character-led era. White Dwarf issues 166 to 225.

editions.name: 3rd Edition
editions.launch_box_note: Softback rulebook plus two forces. Marked the debut of the Dark Eldar as the antagonist faction.
editions.rules_summary: The most significant overhaul to that point. Replaced the dense 2nd edition charts with a core rules plus exceptions model that all later editions built on. Introduced the Force Organisation Chart and simplified vehicle and psychic rules.
key_changes: New game logo used from 3rd to 8th edition
iconic_models: Tau and Necrons introduced late in the edition
editions.narrative_hook: Streamlined for larger, faster battles. Tau debut coincided with a popularity surge in the United States.

editions.name: 4th Edition
editions.launch_box_note: A beginner focused game in a box with a compact softcover ruleset. Expansion The Battle Rages On added scenarios and units.
editions.rules_summary: A measured revision rather than a rewrite. Backwards compatible with 3rd edition codices, with refined terrain, cover and background content. Released as hardback rulebook, the Battle for Macragge boxed set, and a Collector's Edition.
iconic_models: Tyranid swarm from Battle for Macragge
editions.narrative_hook: Set during the Tyranid invasion of Macragge, the First Tyrannic War.

editions.name: 5th Edition
editions.launch_box_note: First starter set to include proper armies rather than a squad or two per side. Each side had an HQ, troops and more, with the Ork Warboss, Nobz and Deffkoptas long exclusive to this box.
editions.rules_summary: Shared the 4th edition core but added major changes. True Line of Sight made terrain placement critical, the Run rule gave infantry flexibility, and transports became central to the competitive meta.
key_changes: Mechanised transport heavy meta (Rhino Rush, Razorback spam)
iconic_models: Assault on Black Reach Ork Warboss and Deffkoptas
editions.narrative_hook: Competitive 40k matured significantly during this era.

editions.name: 6th Edition
editions.launch_box_note: Replaced Assault on Black Reach. Early copies included a limited edition Interrogator-Chaplain for the Dark Angels.
editions.rules_summary: Expanded army building with the Allies system and Fortifications, and added Warlord Traits, expanded psychic powers, Flyers and Overwatch with greater scenery interaction.
iconic_models: Dark Vengeance Dark Angels (Ravenwing and Deathwing elements)
editions.narrative_hook: Crimson Slaughter Chaos warband versus the Dark Angels.

editions.name: 7th Edition
editions.launch_box: Dark Vengeance (carried over); later Stormclaw and other battleboxes; Battle for Vedros entry set
editions.launch_box_note: Reused the Dark Vengeance starter with updated rules. Themed battleforce boxes set you up with specific armies, and Battle for Vedros reused the 5th edition Assault on Black Reach sprues as a low cost entry point.
editions.rules_summary: Very similar to 6th edition but reorganised army building into Battle-forged and Unbound, reworked the psychic phase, added Maelstrom of War missions and folded Lords of War into the standard chart. Suffered heavy rules bloat from formations and supplements.
key_changes: Lords of War in standard play
iconic_models: Genestealer Cults range return and new Deathwatch
editions.narrative_hook: Fall of Cadia, Gathering Storm and Fracture of Biel-Tan advanced the storyline for the first time in decades, ending the static timeline and setting up 8th edition.

editions.name: 8th Edition
editions.launch_box_note: Launched the Primaris Space Marines. The regular marines became known as Firstborn.
editions.rules_summary: The most radical revision since 3rd edition. Introduced Three Ways to Play (Open, Matched, Narrative), a 14 page free core rules PDF, universal datasheets, Command Points and Stratagems, and wounds based vehicles. Introduced the Primaris Space Marines, the Crossing the Rubicon Primaris moment.
key_changes: Three Ways to Play (Open, Matched, Narrative)
editions.narrative_hook: The Plague Wars and the Indomitus Crusade following the Gathering Storm.

editions.name: 9th Edition
editions.launch_box_note: A limited release launch box, followed by the Recruit, Elite and Command edition starter sets. Featured a revised Necron range and new Primaris units.
editions.rules_summary: A minor modification of 8th edition rather than a total overhaul. Codices, supplements and Psychic Awakening content stayed compatible. Added the Crusade narrative campaign system, overhauled terrain rules and secondary objectives, and brought the first logo redesign since 3rd edition.
editions.narrative_hook: Continued the Indomitus Crusade across the galaxy split by the Great Rift.

editions.name: 10th Edition
editions.launch_box_note: Marine versus Tyranid launch box with a large new Tyranid range and updated Space Marine kits.
editions.rules_summary: Another major simplification. Removed Force Organisation charts, gave each army a single Detachment that defines its playstyle with bespoke Enhancements and Stratagems, tightened Stratagems toward a core set, and made free Index rules available for every unit on day one.
key_changes: Free Index rules for every unit on day one
iconic_models: Leviathan Tyranids (Norn Emissary, Von Ryan's Leapers, Neurogaunts)
editions.narrative_hook: The 4th Tyrannic War. Significant changes to the setting of the 41st Millennium.

editions.name: 11th Edition
editions.launch_box_note: Billed as the biggest 40k launch box ever. About 295 USD, 61 push fit miniatures (23 Space Marines and 38 Orks across 12 kits), Core Rules booklet, Operation Imperator lore book, two mission and campaign decks, datasheet cards and transfers.
editions.rules_summary: Framed as an evolution of 10th rather than a ground up rewrite. Existing 10th edition codexes and recent campaign books stay legal at launch. Detachments become modular and combinable using a Detachment Point pool (each detachment costs 1 to 3 DP, 2 DP at 1000 points, 3 DP at 2000 points) with over 70 new and updated detachments. Objective circles are replaced by terrain based objectives, with new rules including Stay Hidden and reworked Fire Overwatch and Snap Shooting. Free core rules PDF and app.
key_changes: Evolution of 10th, codexes remain legal at launch
Modular combinable Detachments with Detachment Points
Terrain based objectives replace objective circles
Stay Hidden rule, reworked Overwatch and Snap Shooting
iconic_models: New Librarian, Chaplain with Jump Pack, Ancient with Armageddon banner
Ork Big Mek Dakkarig and new Boyz
editions.narrative_hook: The war for Armageddon after the Return of Yarrick. Wazdakka Gutsmek lands first, Ghazghkull's main force closes in, and Yarrick triggers Operation Imperator with Blood Angels leading the Imperial coalition.
